insight: The 'first Odin beats second Odin' rule is considered situationally true rather than universal: it holds more reliably when there is strong supporting pressure (e.g., Manticore, Grenade Mech, Drake) but is questionable in most Odin games without such support.
author: 307th
context: Community debate over whether 'first Odin beats second Odin' is a reliable strategic rule.

insight: Odin is considered overpowered specifically when paired with other blue pressure units (Grenade Mech, Drake) or units like Lancetooth and Manticore, because pressure multiplies in value and the first Odin can snowball the opponent; otherwise it is merely a strong unit.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion of Odin's power level and the conditions under which it becomes dominant.

insight: Steelsplitter is widely considered the worst attacker in the game and one of the worst base-set units overall: at 6B it is an inefficient wall and a weak attacker, and is only bought to avoid wasting blue resources after purchasing Blastforges for better units. It is sometimes skipped entirely.
author: consensus
context: Extended discussion comparing Steelsplitter to Tarsier and evaluating its role in the base set.

insight: When you have a Steelsplitter, the correct default play is to click it and buy a Wall rather than hold the Splitter back for defense, since using it as a blocker (6B for defense) is very inefficient. Holding it back is only correct to avoid holding excess resources or to exploit a specific attack opportunity.
author: steel0229e
context: Discussion of Steelsplitter's optimal use and its role as a non-prompt attacker versus defensive unit.

insight: Tarsier has build time 2 whereas Steelsplitter attacks the next turn; this build-time difference is a significant factor in their relative costs. Tarsier is more efficient early game when gold is valuable, but Steelsplitter becomes relatively better in high-pressure situations where gold is worth less and defense is worth more.
author: steel0229e
context: Comparison of Tarsier and Steelsplitter cost-efficiency across different game phases.

insight: Steelsplitter is intentionally weak because Wall is so strong defensively, and Rhino is intentionally weak because Tarsier and red attackers are so strong; this design forces players to mix colors to get optimal results.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion of base-set design philosophy and why certain units are deliberately weak.

insight: Absorb mechanics are intentionally imbalanced in Prismata to prevent every game from devolving into a low-economy rush; the whole game is balanced around the base set's absorb structure.
author: steel0229e
context: Discussion of why Wall at 5B might seem overpowered and the role of absorb in the base set design.

insight: Deadeye Operative's value is approximately equivalent to two Drones over three turns plus 2 absorb: it costs 5B and reduces the opponent's economy by 3 gold over 3 turns (with a time cost), after which it functions as a 2-health soak unit. Its advantage over simply buying Drones is situational and depends on the set.
author: liadahlia
context: Analysis of Deadeye Operative's economic value compared to buying Drones directly.

insight: Deadeye Operative is strong because it forces the opponent to spend more gold on defense than they would if you simply bought attackers, but this advantage is time-delayed over 3 turns rather than immediate, so its value is highly set-dependent.
author: spiritfryer
context: Community discussion on what makes Deadeye Operative good and when to buy it.

insight: Kinetic Driver's snipe ability is considered undervalued by at least one developer (elyot): an early snipe costs 1 damage next turn and some upfront resources, but depriving the opponent of B or RR can cause large inefficiency if their build is weak to it. However, it is frequently played around (opponent holds excess green, switches to a more expensive unit), limiting its impact.
author: elyot
context: Expert debate on the value of the kinetic snipe ability versus its cost.

insight: The most impactful use of Kinetic Driver's snipe is to prevent the opponent from building a key tech structure (e.g., sniping to delay a Defence Grid or deny an Antima Comet), rather than sniping a Conduit, which a calculation showed was worse value than a Gauss Cannon.
author: steel0229e
context: Discussion of when Kinetic Driver snipe is worth using, with a replay example of sniping to deny Antima Comet.

insight: Thunderhead is identified as the dominant 'overpowered' unit in a set when no other unit matches its raw power level; the recommended approach is to rush Thunderhead before the opponent can stabilize, leveraging the synergy that acquiring an Animus early (e.g., turn 1) can create up to 7 damage when combined with Thunderhead.
author: punf
context: Set-reading discussion where punf identifies Thunderhead rush as the correct line when Thunderhead is the strongest unit in the set.

insight: 'Sniping' in Prismata refers to units that kill a specific opposing unit outright (e.g., Apollo, Kinetic Driver/Deadeye Operative), distinct from 'chill' (which freezes/delays a unit) and from units like Tatsu Nullifier or Nivo Charge that interact with units in other ways.
author: consensus
context: Community clarification of terminology during discussion of Apollo and Kinetic Driver.
